were a [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") team that played in [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee"), from 1953 to 1965, having previously played in [Boston, Massachusetts](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), as the [Boston Braves](/wiki/Boston_Braves "Boston Braves"). After relocating to [Atlanta, Georgia](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta") in 1966, they were renamed the [Atlanta Braves](/wiki/Atlanta_Braves "Atlanta Braves").[[4]](#cite_note-5) The 13-season tenure in Milwaukee at [Milwaukee County Stadium](/wiki/Milwaukee_County_Stadium "Milwaukee County Stadium") saw varying degrees of success for the franchise, winning the [1957 World Series](/wiki/1957_World_Series "1957 World Series") and the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") pennant in [1958](/wiki/1958_Major_League_Baseball_season "1958 Major League Baseball season"). The team never finished with a losing record.

The **Milwaukee Braves** were a [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") team that played in [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee"), from 1953 to 1965, having previously played in [Boston, Massachusetts](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), as the [Boston Braves](/wiki/Boston_Braves "Boston Braves"). After relocating to [Atlanta, Georgia](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta") in 1966, they were renamed the [Atlanta Braves](/wiki/Atlanta_Braves "Atlanta Braves").[[4]](#cite_note-5) The 13-season tenure in Milwaukee at [Milwaukee County Stadium](/wiki/Milwaukee_County_Stadium "Milwaukee County Stadium") saw varying degrees of success for the franchise, winning the [1957 World Series](/wiki/1957_World_Series "1957 World Series") and the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") pennant in [1958](/wiki/1958_Major_League_Baseball_season "1958 Major League Baseball season"). The team never finished with a losing record.

The Milwaukee Braves had an overall win–loss record of 1,146–890–8 (.563) during their 13 years in Milwaukee. Three former Milwaukee Braves players were elected to the [National Baseball Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Baseball_Hall_of_Fame "National Baseball Hall of Fame").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Relocation from Boston

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Relocation from Boston")]

Construction began on [Milwaukee County Stadium](/wiki/Milwaukee_County_Stadium "Milwaukee County Stadium") in 1950 in hopes of both luring a Major League baseball team, as well as the [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League"). The minor league [Milwaukee Brewers](/wiki/Milwaukee_Brewers_(American_Association) "Milwaukee Brewers (American Association)") were scheduled to begin play at the start of the 1953 season.[[5]](#cite_note-Story_Behind_This_1953_Brewers_Ticket2-6)

However, in the first move of a Major League team in half a century, on March 18, 1953, the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") approved owner [Lou Perini's](/wiki/Lou_Perini "Lou Perini") move of the [Boston Braves](/wiki/Boston_Braves "Boston Braves") to Milwaukee 8–0 because of his "fine standing" with the other owners and also because there was an open city for his minor league team then in Milwaukee. The minor league Brewers moved to [Toledo, Ohio](/wiki/Toledo,_Ohio "Toledo, Ohio"), and changed their name to the [Mudhens](/wiki/Toledo_Mud_Hens "Toledo Mud Hens"). The full AP quote about fine standing: "Warren Giles, National League president, repeated again and again that 'Perini's fine standing with the other club owners was the most important reason for their approval.'"[[5]](#cite_note-Story_Behind_This_1953_Brewers_Ticket2-6)

Milwaukee County gave the Braves a favorable stadium deal. For the first two years, the team would pay only $1,000 a year for the use of Milwaukee County Stadium. For the next three years, the team would pay 5% of ticket prices and concessions. After that, the rent would be negotiated afresh, with the Braves being required to open their books.[[6]](#cite_note-AP_news_article,_19532-7)

### 1953–1959: World Series and near-successes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1953–1959: World Series and near-successes")]

[Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee") went wild over the Braves, who were welcomed as genuine heroes. The [Braves](/wiki/1953_Milwaukee_Braves_season "1953 Milwaukee Braves season") finished 92–62 (.597) in their [first season](/wiki/1953_Milwaukee_Braves_season "1953 Milwaukee Braves season") in Milwaukee, and drew a then-NL record 1.8 million fans.[[7]](#cite_note-bfsnlmk-8)[[8]](#cite_note-ftnlre-9) The success of the team was noted by many owners. Not coincidentally, the [Philadelphia Athletics](/wiki/Philadelphia_Athletics "Philadelphia Athletics"), [St. Louis Browns](/wiki/St._Louis_Browns "St. Louis Browns"), [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/Brooklyn_Dodgers "Brooklyn Dodgers"), and [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants_(baseball) "New York Giants (baseball)") all relocated over the next five years.

As the 1950s progressed, the reinvigorated Braves became increasingly competitive. Sluggers [Eddie Mathews](/wiki/Eddie_Mathews "Eddie Mathews") and [Hank Aaron](/wiki/Hank_Aaron "Hank Aaron") drove the offense (they would hit a combined 1,226 home runs as Braves, with 850 of those coming while the franchise was in Milwaukee), while [Warren Spahn](/wiki/Warren_Spahn "Warren Spahn"), [Lew Burdette](/wiki/Lew_Burdette "Lew Burdette"), and [Bob Buhl](/wiki/Bob_Buhl "Bob Buhl") anchored the rotation.

#### 1953

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1953")]

Main article: [1953 Milwaukee Braves season](/wiki/1953_Milwaukee_Braves_season "1953 Milwaukee Braves season")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Milwaukee_Braves_1953_welcome_parade.jpg/330px-Milwaukee_Braves_1953_welcome_parade.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee_Braves_1953_welcome_parade.jpg)

Residents of Milwaukee line the streets to welcome the arrival of the Braves franchise on April 8, 1953.

In the inaugural season of the Braves in Milwaukee, the team found success early on. For the latter half of May and most of June, the Braves held a narrow lead in the National League. By the June 15 trade deadline, the team was 37–18 (.673) and 0 games behind the [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/1953_Brooklyn_Dodgers_season "1953 Brooklyn Dodgers season"), who were only ahead by percentage points, with a record of 36–17 (.679). Their widest lead was on June 23 and 24, 3 games over the Dodgers, though the team would quickly lose their lead just 3 days later and never recovered.[[9]](#cite_note-10) The Dodgers went on to outperform the rest of the league as the Braves finished in second, at 92–62 (.597) and 13 games behind.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

#### 1954

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1954")]

Main article: [1954 Milwaukee Braves season](/wiki/1954_Milwaukee_Braves_season "1954 Milwaukee Braves season")

The sophomore season of the Braves in Milwaukee saw less success than the previous season. Though the team went on a 10-game win streak towards the end of May, they only led the National League for the last week of May, at most 1½ games ahead. Following a losing record in June, the team never recovered, and dropped to 15½ games behind on July 14 and 21. The team would remain in third place for almost the entirety of the remaining season, even when a 9-game win streak put the team only 3½ games behind the eventual [World Series](/wiki/1954_World_Series "1954 World Series") winning [New York Giants](/wiki/1954_New_York_Giants_(MLB)_season "1954 New York Giants (MLB) season") by August 15. The team finished the season 89–65 (.578) and 8 games behind.[[11]](#cite_note-12)

#### 1955

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1955")]

Main article: [1955 Milwaukee Braves season](/wiki/1955_Milwaukee_Braves_season "1955 Milwaukee Braves season")

The 1955 team saw even less success than the previous season under new general manager [John Quinn](/wiki/John_Quinn_(baseball_executive) "John Quinn (baseball executive)") and new manager [Charlie Grimm](/wiki/Charlie_Grimm "Charlie Grimm"). By May 28, the team was 10½ games behind, and would never be closer than 10 games behind for the remainder of the season, even though they held second place in the National League from July 7 until the season's end, trailing the eventual [World Series](/wiki/1955_World_Series "1955 World Series") winning [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/1955_Brooklyn_Dodgers "1955 Brooklyn Dodgers"). The team finished the season 85–69 (.552) and 13½ games behind.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

#### 1956

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1956")]

Main article: [1956 Milwaukee Braves season](/wiki/1956_Milwaukee_Braves_season "1956 Milwaukee Braves season")

Under opening day manager [Charlie Grimm](/wiki/Charlie_Grimm "Charlie Grimm"), the Braves got off to a mediocre start at 24–22 (.522). After a loss on Saturday, June 16, the owners dismissed him and replaced him with [Fred Haney](/wiki/Fred_Haney "Fred Haney"), who led the Braves to a 68–40 (.630) record for the rest of the season, leading for the majority of the season. For the second half of July, all of August, and most of September, the Braves held a small lead in the National League, in a tight race with the [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/1956_Brooklyn_Dodgers_season "1956 Brooklyn Dodgers season") and [Cincinnati Redlegs](/wiki/1956_Cincinnati_Redlegs_season "1956 Cincinnati Redlegs season"). Going into the last series of the season, the Braves held a ½ game lead over the Dodgers. In losing the series 1–2 to the [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/1956_St._Louis_Cardinals_season "1956 St. Louis Cardinals season"), they choked the season and finished at 92–62 (.597), 1 game behind the Dodgers, who finished at 93–61 (.604).[[13]](#cite_note-14)

#### 1957

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1957")]

Main article: [1957 Milwaukee Braves season](/wiki/1957_Milwaukee_Braves_season "1957 Milwaukee Braves season")

The first half of the 1957 season saw the Braves in-and-out of first place, mostly between them and the [Cincinnati Redlegs](/wiki/1957_Cincinnati_Redlegs_season "1957 Cincinnati Redlegs season") and [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/1957_St._Louis_Cardinals_season "1957 St. Louis Cardinals season"). August 7 saw the Braves take the first place in the National League, a lead they would hold for the rest of the season, finishing at 95–59 (.617).[[14]](#cite_note-15) The team celebrated their first pennant in nine years. Spearheaded by [Hank Aaron's](/wiki/Hank_Aaron "Hank Aaron") [MVP season](/wiki/MLB_Most_Valuable_Player_Award "MLB Most Valuable Player Award"), he led the National League in home runs and RBI. Perhaps the most memorable of his 44 round-trippers that season came on September 23, a two-run [walk-off home run](/wiki/Walk-off_home_run "Walk-off home run") that gave the Braves a 4–2 victory over the [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/1957_St._Louis_Cardinals_season "1957 St. Louis Cardinals season") and clinched the league championship. The team drew over 2.2 million at home during the regular season, then went on to its first [World Series](/wiki/1957_World_Series "1957 World Series") win in over 40 years, defeating the [New York Yankees](/wiki/1957_New_York_Yankees_season "1957 New York Yankees season") of [Yogi Berra](/wiki/Yogi_Berra "Yogi Berra"), [Mickey Mantle](/wiki/Mickey_Mantle "Mickey Mantle"), and [Whitey Ford](/wiki/Whitey_Ford "Whitey Ford") in seven games. Burdette, the [Series MVP](/wiki/World_Series_Most_Valuable_Player "World Series Most Valuable Player"), threw three complete game victories, giving up only two earned runs, including the seventh game in New York, a 5–0 shutout.[[15]](#cite_note-bwwti-16)[[16]](#cite_note-bithwrl-17)

#### 1958

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 1958")]

Main article: [1958 Milwaukee Braves season](/wiki/1958_Milwaukee_Braves_season "1958 Milwaukee Braves season")

The first half of the 1958 season saw the Braves in-and-out of first place, mostly between them and the newly relocated [San Francisco Giants](/wiki/1958_San_Francisco_Giants_season "1958 San Francisco Giants season"). The Braves would secure first place for the rest of the season from July 30, finishing the season 92–62 (.597), 8 games ahead, securing their second consecutive National League pennant.[[17]](#cite_note-18) In the [World Series](/wiki/1958_World_Series "1958 World Series"), the Braves jumped out to a three games to one lead in against New York once more, thanks in part to the strength of [Warren Spahn](/wiki/Warren_Spahn "Warren Spahn")'s and [Lew Burdette](/wiki/Lew_Burdette "Lew Burdette")'s pitching. But the Yankees stormed back to take the last three games, the last two in Milwaukee, in large part to World Series MVP [Bob Turley](/wiki/Bob_Turley "Bob Turley"), the winning pitcher in games five and seven.

#### 1959

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 1959")]

Main article: [1959 Milwaukee Braves season](/wiki/1959_Milwaukee_Braves_season "1959 Milwaukee Braves season")

The [1959](/wiki/1959_Major_League_Baseball_season "1959 Major League Baseball season") season, under new general manager [John McHale](/wiki/John_McHale_(baseball) "John McHale (baseball)"), saw a tight three-way race between the Braves, [Los Angeles Dodgers](/wiki/1959_Los_Angeles_Dodgers_season "1959 Los Angeles Dodgers season"), [San Francisco Giants](/wiki/1959_San_Francisco_Giants_season "1959 San Francisco Giants season") for the pennant race. The Braves led the National League in May and June, whereas in July and August, the team hovered at its worst, 4½ games behind. The three-way race continued into the last week of September, when the Giants fell off (having led in July and August). By the season's end, both the Braves and Dodgers were tied at 86–68 (.558), resulting in a [regular season best-of-three tie-breaker series](/wiki/1959_National_League_tie-breaker_series "1959 National League tie-breaker series") to determine the winner of the pennant.

Many residents of Chicago and Milwaukee were hoping for a [White Sox](/wiki/1959_Chicago_White_Sox_season "1959 Chicago White Sox season")–Braves World Series, as the cities are only about 75 miles (120 km) apart along the west shore of [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan"). However, it was not to be. Though consistently behind both the Braves and Giants all season, the Dodgers won the league title with two straight wins against the Braves, ending the Braves' pennant streak at two.[[18]](#cite_note-cblach-19) The Dodgers would go on to defeat the White Sox in six games in the [World Series](/wiki/1959_World_Series "1959 World Series"). The Braves finished their season at 86–70 (.551).[[19]](#cite_note-20)

### 1960–1965: Declining attendance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 1960–1965: Declining attendance")]

The next six years were up-and-down for the Braves. The Braves were somewhat mediocre as the 1960s began but fattened up on the expansion [New York Mets](/wiki/New_York_Mets "New York Mets") and [Houston Colt .45s](/wiki/Houston_Colt_.45s "Houston Colt .45s") (now the American League Astros) starting in 1962.

#### 1960

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 1960")]

Main article: [1960 Milwaukee Braves season](/wiki/1960_Milwaukee_Braves_season "1960 Milwaukee Braves season")

The [1960](/wiki/1960_Major_League_Baseball_season "1960 Major League Baseball season") season under new manager [Chuck Dressen](/wiki/Chuck_Dressen "Chuck Dressen") saw the Braves somewhat fall of from the previous season, but for vast majority of the season, the team would place in second or third, always within 8 games behind. The closest the team was to leading was on July 24, when they were at 52–36 (.591) and 0 games ahead of the [Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/1960_Pittsburgh_Pirates_season "1960 Pittsburgh Pirates season"), though were ahead by percentage (the Pirates were at 53–37 (.589)). From August 6 on, the Braves were consistently between 5 and 8 games behind and finished the season in second place at 88–66 (.571), 7 games behind the [World Series](/wiki/1960_World_Series "1960 World Series") winning [Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/1960_Pittsburgh_Pirates_season "1960 Pittsburgh Pirates season").[[20]](#cite_note-21) Two players threw no-hitters against the [Philadelphia Phillies](/wiki/1960_Philadelphia_Phillies_season "1960 Philadelphia Phillies season"): [Lew Burdette](/wiki/Lew_Burdette "Lew Burdette") on August 18 and [Warren Spahn](/wiki/Warren_Spahn "Warren Spahn") on September 16.[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23) Milwaukee's home attendance slipped under 1.5 million for the first time since the move from Boston.

#### 1961

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 1961")]

Main article: [1961 Milwaukee Braves season](/wiki/1961_Milwaukee_Braves_season "1961 Milwaukee Braves season")

The [1961](/wiki/1961_Major_League_Baseball_season "1961 Major League Baseball season") season saw the Braves hover around fourth and fifth for most of the season. After the June 15 trade deadline, the Braves would be consistently between 6 and 14½ games behind, eventually finishing the season in fourth place, at 83–71 (.539), 10 games behind the [Cincinnati Reds](/wiki/1961_Cincinnati_Reds_season "1961 Cincinnati Reds season").[[23]](#cite_note-24) [Warren Spahn](/wiki/Warren_Spahn "Warren Spahn") threw his 300th career victory on August 11 and threw his second no-hitter, this time against the [San Francisco Giants](/wiki/1961_San_Francisco_Giants_season "1961 San Francisco Giants season") on April 28.[[24]](#cite_note-25)[[25]](#cite_note-26) After the game on September 2, manager [Chuck Dressen](/wiki/Chuck_Dressen "Chuck Dressen") was replaced by [Birdie Tebbetts](/wiki/Birdie_Tebbetts "Birdie Tebbetts"). The team's home attendance continued its decline; the last season exceeding one million was in 1961.

#### 1962

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 1962")]

Main article: [1962 Milwaukee Braves season](/wiki/1962_Milwaukee_Braves_season "1962 Milwaukee Braves season")

The [1962](/wiki/1962_Major_League_Baseball_season "1962 Major League Baseball season") season saw the Braves consistently in sixth place from mid-May through mid-September. Before July 22, the team had a negative record, though after this date would have a positive record for the remainder of the season. The team finished the season in fifth at 86–76 (.531), 15½ games behind the [San Francisco Giants](/wiki/1962_San_Francisco_Giants_season "1962 San Francisco Giants season").[[26]](#cite_note-27) [Hank Aaron](/wiki/Hank_Aaron "Hank Aaron") his Milwaukee career high 45 home runs. Attendance fell off significantly below the previous season, falling below 800,000.

#### 1963

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 1963")]

Main article: [1963 Milwaukee Braves season](/wiki/1963_Milwaukee_Braves_season "1963 Milwaukee Braves season")

The [1963](/wiki/1963_Major_League_Baseball_season "1963 Major League Baseball season") season was the first under the ownership of [William Bartholomay](/wiki/William_Bartholomay "William Bartholomay") and new manager [Bobby Bragan](/wiki/Bobby_Bragan "Bobby Bragan"). The Braves performed slightly worse than the previous season. After the All-Star break, the Braves were consistently between 7 and 16 games behind, finishing the season in sixth at 84–78 (.519), 15 games behind the [World Series](/wiki/1963_World_Series "1963 World Series") winning [Los Angeles Dodgers](/wiki/1963_Los_Angeles_Dodgers_season "1963 Los Angeles Dodgers season").[[27]](#cite_note-28) This would be the only season that the Milwaukee Braves finished "[second division](/wiki/First_division_(baseball) "First division (baseball)")", having placed in the lower half of the league. [Hank Aaron](/wiki/Hank_Aaron "Hank Aaron") hit 44 home runs and notched 130 RBI, and [Warren Spahn](/wiki/Warren_Spahn "Warren Spahn") was once again the ace of the staff. There was a slight uptick in attendance, though still below 800,000.

#### 1964

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 1964")]

Main article: [1964 Milwaukee Braves season](/wiki/1964_Milwaukee_Braves_season "1964 Milwaukee Braves season")

The [1964](/wiki/1964_Major_League_Baseball_season "1964 Major League Baseball season") season saw the Braves slightly improve from the previous season. By the All-Star break, the team was 2 games under .500, in seventh place. For the remainder of the season, the Braves were consistently between 5 and 14½ games behind while consistently in fifth or sixth place. The Braves finished the season in fifth at 88–74 (.543), 5 games behind the [World Series](/wiki/1964_World_Series "1964 World Series") winning [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/1964_St._Louis_Cardinals "1964 St. Louis Cardinals").[[28]](#cite_note-29) Despite the consistently lower performance to the previous two seasons, attendance saw a large uptick to over 900,000.

#### 1965

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: 1965")]

Main article: [1965 Milwaukee Braves season](/wiki/1965_Milwaukee_Braves_season "1965 Milwaukee Braves season")

An injunction filed in Wisconsin blocked the Braves from moving to [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta") for the [1965](/wiki/1965_Major_League_Baseball_season "1965 Major League Baseball season") season, but attendance plummeted to 555,000.[[29]](#cite_note-bsfwisb-30) The team saw the Braves perform slightly worse than the previous season. However, in the months of June and August, the team was largely in second place behind the [Los Angeles Dodgers](/wiki/1965_Los_Angeles_Dodgers_season "1965 Los Angeles Dodgers season") by only a few games, having led the National League only on August 18 and 20 by only ½ games ahead. Outside these months, the team hovered between fourth and fifth place. The Braves finished the season in fifth at 86–76 (.531), 11 games behind the [World Series](/wiki/1965_World_Series "1965 World Series") winning Dodgers.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

### Relocation to Atlanta

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Relocation to Atlanta")]

Lou Perini sold the Braves to a [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago")-based group led by [William Bartholomay](/wiki/William_Bartholomay "William Bartholomay") after the 1962 season. The ink had barely dried on the deal when Bartholomay started shopping the Braves to a larger television market. At the same time, the fast-growing city of [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"), led by Mayor [Ivan Allen, Jr.](/wiki/Ivan_Allen,_Jr. "Ivan Allen, Jr."), constructed a new $18 million, 52,007–60,606 seat multi-purpose stadium in less than one year, [Atlanta Stadium](/wiki/Atlanta%E2%80%93Fulton_County_Stadium "Atlanta–Fulton County Stadium") (later on known as Atlanta–Fulton County Stadium from 1976 until 1996 with its demolition in 1997), which was officially opened in 1965 in hopes of luring an existing [major league](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") and/or [NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League")/[AFL](/wiki/American_Football_League "American Football League") team.

After the city failed to lure the [Kansas City Athletics](/wiki/1965_Kansas_City_Athletics_season "1965 Kansas City Athletics season") (who moved to [Oakland](/wiki/Oakland "Oakland") in [1968](/wiki/1968_Oakland_Athletics_season "1968 Oakland Athletics season")), Allen courted the Braves, who announced their intention to move to Atlanta for the [1965](/wiki/1965_Milwaukee_Braves_season "1965 Milwaukee Braves season") season. However, an injunction filed in Wisconsin forced the Braves to play a lame-duck season in Milwaukee, but the home attendance was less than 560,000.[[29]](#cite_note-bsfwisb-30) In the interim, Atlanta Stadium played host to the Braves' new Triple-A affiliate, the [Atlanta Crackers](/wiki/Atlanta_Crackers "Atlanta Crackers") of the [International League](/wiki/International_League "International League"). The Braves had bought the Crackers in order to secure the major-league rights to the Atlanta area; in those days, the owner of a minor-league team also owned the major league rights to that city.

The Braves completed the [move to Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta_Braves "Atlanta Braves") prior to the [1966](/wiki/1966_Atlanta_Braves_season "1966 Atlanta Braves season") season, and drew over 1.5 million in the new stadium that first year.[[31]](#cite_note-btbr-32) Before that season, they moved the Crackers to [Richmond, Virginia](/wiki/Richmond,_Virginia "Richmond, Virginia") as the [Richmond Braves](/wiki/Richmond_Braves "Richmond Braves").

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Legacy")]

Following the team's departure from Milwaukee after the 1965 season, the city was quickly considered for expansion, as stated by [Commissioner of Baseball](/wiki/Commissioner_of_Baseball "Commissioner of Baseball") [William Eckert](/wiki/William_Eckert "William Eckert") in May 1966, citing that expansion would occur in "eight to 10 years."[[32]](#cite_note-33) However, by 1968, Milwaukee was rejected for expansion due to its close proximity to [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago").[[33]](#cite_note-34)

As early as June 1969, an MLB memo envisaged the newly founded [Seattle Pilots](/wiki/Seattle_Pilots "Seattle Pilots") of the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") moving to Milwaukee.[[34]](#cite_note-35) Concerns for the Pilots' viability led to several attempts to relocate the team to Milwaukee, which lead to Pilots owner [Dewey Soriano](/wiki/Dewey_Soriano "Dewey Soriano") to try and sell the team. Following failed attempts to sell to former Braves minority owner [Bud Selig](/wiki/Bud_Selig "Bud Selig") and several deals involving [Westin Hotels](/wiki/Westin_Hotels "Westin Hotels") head [Eddie Carlson](/wiki/Edward_Carlson "Edward Carlson"), and bankruptcy of the team on March 31—seven days before Opening Day—the team was sold to Selig and the team was moved to Milwaukee as the [Milwaukee Brewers](/wiki/Milwaukee_Brewers "Milwaukee Brewers"). The [National League's](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") 32-year hiatus from the city would end in [1998](/wiki/1998_Major_League_Baseball_season "1998 Major League Baseball season") when the Brewers were transferred to the National League due to realignment resulting from the [1998 Major League Baseball expansion](/wiki/1998_Major_League_Baseball_expansion "1998 Major League Baseball expansion").

## Uniforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Uniforms")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/MIL1964H41MATHEWS.jpg/500px-MIL1964H41MATHEWS.jpg)](/wiki/File:MIL1964H41MATHEWS.jpg)

1964 Milwaukee Braves [Eddie Mathews](/wiki/Eddie_Mathews "Eddie Mathews") uniform.

When the team moved to Milwaukee, their uniforms were largely unchanged. The home uniforms were plain white with thin piping going around the collar and down the middle of shirt. Red script "Braves" was across the chest with navy trim. At various times, there was piping around each sleeve. Piping on the uniform could be red or navy depending on the year. Road uniforms were the same format, but on a gray shirt.

## Notable Milwaukee Braves

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_Braves&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Notable Milwaukee Braves")]

See also: [Milwaukee Braves Wall of Honor](/wiki/Milwaukee_Braves_Wall_of_Honor "Milwaukee Braves Wall of Honor")

* [Hank Aaron](/wiki/Hank_Aaron "Hank Aaron"), a [right fielder](/wiki/Right_fielder "Right fielder") on the team from 1954 until the team's departure to Atlanta after 1965. He is considered the team's most accomplished player. He was inducted into the [Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Baseball_Hall_of_Fame_and_Museum "National Baseball Hall of Fame and Museum") in his first year of eligibility in 1982.
* [Joe Adcock](/wiki/Joe_Adcock "Joe Adcock"), a [first baseman](/wiki/First_baseman "First baseman") on the team from 1953 to 1962.
* [Frank Bolling](/wiki/Frank_Bolling "Frank Bolling"), a [second baseman](/wiki/Second_baseman "Second baseman") on the team from 1961 to after the team's 1966 departure to Atlanta.
* [Bill Bruton](/wiki/Bill_Bruton "Bill Bruton"), a [center fielder](/wiki/Center_fielder "Center fielder") on the team from 1953 to 1960.
* [Bob Buhl](/wiki/Bob_Buhl "Bob Buhl"), a [pitcher](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher") on the team from 1953 to 1962.
* [Lew Burdette](/wiki/Lew_Burdette "Lew Burdette"), a [pitcher](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher") on the team from 1953 to 1963.
* [Gene Conley](/wiki/Gene_Conley "Gene Conley"), a [pitcher](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher") on the team from 1954 to 1958.
* [Wes Covington](/wiki/Wes_Covington "Wes Covington"), a [left fielder](/wiki/Left_fielder "Left fielder") on the team from 1956 to 1961.
* [Del Crandall](/wiki/Del_Crandall "Del Crandall"), a [catcher](/wiki/Catcher "Catcher") on the team from before the team's 1953 arrival to Milwaukee to 1963.
* [Johnny Logan](/wiki/Johnny_Logan_(baseball) "Johnny Logan (baseball)"), a [shortstop](/wiki/Shortstop "Shortstop") on the team from before the team's 1953 arrival to Milwaukee to 1961
* [Félix Mantilla](/wiki/F%C3%A9lix_Mantilla_(baseball) "Félix Mantilla (baseball)"), a [second baseman](/wiki/Second_baseman "Second baseman") on the team from 1956 to 1961.
* [Eddie Mathews](/wiki/Eddie_Mathews "Eddie Mathews"), a [third baseman](/wiki/Third_baseman "Third baseman") on the team from before the team's 1953 arrival to Milwaukee to after the team's 1966 departure to Atlanta, and is the only such player to have played on the Braves three home cities. He was inducted into the [Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Baseball_Hall_of_Fame_and_Museum "National Baseball Hall of Fame and Museum") in 1978.
* [Andy Pafko](/wiki/Andy_Pafko "Andy Pafko"), a [right fielder](/wiki/Right_fielder "Right fielder") on the team from 1953 to 1959.
* [Red Schoendienst](/wiki/Red_Schoendienst "Red Schoendienst"), a [second baseman](/wiki/Second_baseman "Second baseman") on the team from 1957 to 1960. He was inducted into the [Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Baseball_Hall_of_Fame_and_Museum "National Baseball Hall of Fame and Museum") in 1989.
* [Warren Spahn](/wiki/Warren_Spahn "Warren Spahn"), a [pitcher](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher") on the team from before the team's 1953 arrival to 1964. He was inducted into the [Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Baseball_Hall_of_Fame_and_Museum "National Baseball Hall of Fame and Museum") in his first year of eligibility in 1973. The [Warren Spahn Award](/wiki/Warren_Spahn_Award "Warren Spahn Award"), given annually to the major leagues' best left-handed pitcher, is named in his honor.
* [Bobby Thomson](/wiki/Bobby_Thomson "Bobby Thomson"), a [left fielder](/wiki/Left_fielder "Left fielder") on the team from 1954 to 1957.
* [Frank Torre](/wiki/Frank_Torre "Frank Torre"), a [first baseman](/wiki/First_baseman "First baseman") on the team from 1956 to 1960.
* [Joe Torre](/wiki/Joe_Torre "Joe Torre"), a [catcher](/wiki/Catcher "Catcher") on the team from 1960 to after the team's 1966 departure to Atlanta. He was inducted into the [Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Baseball_Hall_of_Fame_and_Museum "National Baseball Hall of Fame and Museum") in 2014.
* [Bob Uecker](/wiki/Bob_Uecker "Bob Uecker"), a [catcher](/wiki/Catcher "Catcher") on the team from 1962 to 1963. He was awarded the [Ford C. Frick Award](/wiki/Ford_C._Frick_Award "Ford C. Frick Award") in 2003 by the [National Baseball Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Baseball_Hall_of_Fame_and_Museum "National Baseball Hall of Fame and Museum").
